EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent moist west to northwesterly flow brought heavy snow to portions of the western mountains. The heaviest fell at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort were 28 inches of new snow fell. Amounts dropped sharply at lower elevations and other SNOTEL sites. In the Salt and Wyoming Range, 21 inches was measured at Commissary Ridge. Like in the Tetons, other areas saw much less snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interstate 80 closed for several hours into the evening and overnight period from a crash shortly after 10 AM MDT near the Lone Tree exit about two miles west of Buford. Around 10:15 AM MDT, a Wyoming Highway Patrol officer stopped to assist a stranded commercial trailer truck stuck in the roadway. As the officer was speaking to the driver, another oncoming vehicle ran into the rear of the stopped truck. Additional following vehicles lost control and crashed into each other. In total, over twenty-seven vehicles were involved including twenty-three commercial trucks. Those who were injured were transported for medical treatment. During the time of the crash, freezing fog conditions with an issued freezing fog advisory were present.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 80 closed for several hours into the evening and overnight period from a crash shortly after 10 AM MDT near the Lone Tree exit about two miles west of Buford. Around 10:15 AM MDT, a Wyoming Highway Patrol officer stopped to assist a stranded commercial trailer truck stuck in the roadway. As the officer was speaking to the driver, another oncoming vehicle ran into the rear of the stopped truck. Additional following vehicles lost control and crashed into each other. In total, over twenty-seven vehicles were involved including twenty-three commercial trucks. Those who were injured were transported for medical treatment. During the time of the crash, freezing fog conditions with an issued freezing fog advisory were present. No official injury count to accurately report in stormdata.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist, southwest Pacific flow combined with favorable upper level dynamics and upslope flow, resulted in heavy snow developing in portions of northwestern Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in the Tetons where over two feet was common, including 27 inches at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. The heavy snow moved into portions of the Jackson Valley as well with over a foot of new snow in some locations. Over a foot of snow also fell in portions of southwestern Yellowstone Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in southwestern portions of Yellowstone Park. The highest amount recorded was 15 inches at the Lewis Lake Divide SNOTEL site.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell throughout the Tetons. One of the more notable amounts was 27 inches at the top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL LARAMIE RANGE AND SOUTHWEST PLATTE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong high wind pattern especially through the gap wind areas and high plain adjacent areas of southeastern Wyoming were present the 2nd through late on the 4th of March. Several Wyoming Department of Transportation sensors along with Union Pacific Railroad sensors measured high wind gusts from the upper 50s into the low 80s during these three days. Much of interstate travel was affected for high profile, low weight vehicles and those with trailers as the conditions dictated that they would be unable to travel through these areas still winds finally diminished.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor along Interstate 25 at Bordeaux or nine miles south of Wheatland measured the reported wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick period of snow under twenty-four hours dropped heavy snow across portions of southeastern Wyoming on the 1st. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave and Pacific moisture combined to produce severe weather across portions of western and central Wyoming. The worst damage was in Afton, Wyoming, where a microburst occurred. A wind gust of 60 mph was reported at the Afton, Wyoming airport. Numerous trees were blown down in the Star Valley, and there were numerous reports of power outages in the Star Valley. Further northeast, golf ball size hail was reported at Buffalo, Wyoming and nickel sized hail at Riverton, Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS Employee reported nickel sized hail near Riverton, Wyoming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall occurred over portions of Converse and Niobrara Counties in Wyoming including the cities of Douglas and Glenrock where reports of up to six inches of snowfall were measured. Areas of freezing drizzle along the Interstate 80 corridor created areas of accumulation that fell below quarter of an inch with mild commerce impacts to note directly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received reports of widespread 4 to 6 inches of snowfall across the county with localized amounts up to 8 inches in Glenrock, WY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief moment of high wind speeds were felt across the South Laramie Range on the 23rd. A Union Pacific Railroad sensor about two miles west of Buford measured a high wind gust of 58 mph. A moisten atmosphere and topographic forcing coupled with synoptic forcing aloft assisted in the accumulation of over a foot of heavy snow along the higher elevations of the Snowy Range Mountains by SNOTEL sensors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Union Pacific Railroad sensor about two miles west of Buford measured the reported high wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season high wind event was measured across the southern portions of the wind gap areas in southeastern Wyoming on the 6th and 7th with peak wind gusts measured at 62 mph. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor along Interstate 80 at Arlington measured the reported wind gust. Several other sensors in the area measured sustained winds above 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a cold front moving in from the northwest and moisture from the south brought locally heavy snow to portions of central Wyoming. Portions of the lower elevations in Fremont and Natrona County received amounts ranging from 6 to 9 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts around Casper, Wyoming ranged from 5 to 8 inches, including 7.8 inches at the Natrona County airport. Less snow fell in northern Natrona County, Wyoming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of an unusually cold air mass, calm wind and fresh snow cover brought very cold temperatures to much of western and central Wyoming. Temperatures as low as 19 below occurred in Yellowstone Park. Several stations had all time record lows for the month of April, including Riverton, Buffalo, Greybull and Worland, Wyoming. The record low temperature at Worland, Wyoming was especially noteworthy, since it broke a record that stood since 1947.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very cold temperatures occurred in Yellowstone Park. The lowest temperature recorded was 19 below zero at Grant Village.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall was measured across much of Niobrara County on the 12th of April. A CoCoRaHS observer near Lusk measured 6.0 inches of snowfall while a public report ten miles west of Mule Creek Junction measured 9.0 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer located about a half mile south of Lusk measured 6.0 inches of snowfall. An additional public report located ten miles west of Mule Creek Junction measured 9.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers, many with no reported lightning associated with them produced wind gusts in excess of 58 mph across portions of southeastern Wyoming. Strongest gust was 68 mph measured near Elk Mountain and Sibley Peak. A thorough review of lightning data was used to assist and verify if lightning was present in and around the time of the reported wind gusts. Deep inverted V soundings were present in the hours ahead of the event, an indicative sign of strong mixing and wind speeds possible with these cellular storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor located about eight miles south of Sibley Peak measured the reported wind gust. A review of available lightning data showed no lightning associated with the parent cell that produced these winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm moved across the region, bringing significant snowfall to much of northeastern Wyoming. Rain changed to snow late in the afternoon on the 1st, with snowfall continuing through much of the night. Snowfall amounts of four to eight inches were reported across much of the area, with as much as 12 inches over the Wyoming Black Hills. Gusty winds produced some blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving, but potent, upper level disturbance brought snow to far northeastern Wyoming. Snowfall of two to five inches fell across Crook County, especially over the Bear Lodge Mountains.
Cold advection following a cold front passage brought high wind to northern Johnson County. The highest wind gust recorded was 75 mph at the Buffalo Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred in northern Johnson County. The strongest wind gust recorded was 75 mph at the Johnson County Airport in Buffalo, Wyoming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Canadian cold front moving in from the north combined with Pacific moisture moving in from the south and brought heavy snow to portions of northern and central Wyoming. In the mountains, amounts of over a foot were recorded in the Big Horn and Wind River Range. In the lower Elevations, the highest amounts fell in Fremont County where close to a foot fell around Lander with 6 to 9 inches around Riverton, Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the Big Horn Mountains. At the Middle Powder SNOTEL site, 12 inches of new snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short period of high winds were measured by various Wyoming Department of Transportation sensors along Interstate 80 on the 4th. Highest wind speed measured 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor along Interstate 80 at Elk Mountain measured the reported wind speed. Another sensor at Halleck Ridge also measured into the low 60 mph wind gust range albeit lower than the reported for Elk Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Potent low pressure system pass through the region the evening of the 8th with cold Canadian air mixing in behind cold front while ample moisture source proved conducive to warning criteria snowfall amounts in the Laramie Valley. Up to eight inches of snowfall fell in the city of Laramie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various observers across the city of Laramie measured over 6 inches of snowfall with the highest reliable accumulation measuring 8.0 inches. Several tree limbs snapped under the weight of the snowfall across the city requiring assistance from the city of Cheyenne to send there tree removal crews due to high number of cases and to remove the hazards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers, many with no reported lightning associated with them produced wind gusts in excess of 58 mph across portions of southeastern Wyoming. Strongest gust was 68 mph measured near Elk Mountain and Sibley Peak. A thorough review of lightning data was used to assist and verify if lightning was present in and around the time of the reported wind gusts. Deep inverted V soundings were present in the hours ahead of the event, an indicative sign of strong mixing and wind speeds possible with these cellular storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor along Interstate 80 about nine miles south of Hanna or nine miles west-northwest of Elk Mountain measured the reported wind gust. A review of available lightning data showed no lightning associated with the parent cell that produced these winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Canadian cold front moving in from the north combined with Pacific moisture moving in from the south and brought heavy snow to portions of northern and central Wyoming. In the mountains, amounts of over a foot were recorded in the Big Horn and Wind River Range. In the lower Elevations, the highest amounts fell in Fremont County where close to a foot fell around Lander with 6 to 9 inches around Riverton, Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the Wind River Mountains. At the Deer Park SNOTEL site, 13 inches of new snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season, quick hitting heavy snow from a digging low pressure system in the lee of the Rockies produced widespread heavy snow mainly in the evening and overnight hours across southeastern Wyoming. By sunlight on the 16th, many towns and communities had received snow upwards of six inches and more. The higher terrain areas of the Sierra Madres, Snowy and South Laramie Ranges measured well over a foot of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer located seven miles south-southeast of Yoder measured a total snowfall of 8.1 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow developed ahead of an advancing cold front and brought mixed down some strong wind level winds across portions of Natrona and southeastern Fremont County, Wyoming. The strongest wind occurred at the Natrona County airport where the maximum wind gust was 75 mph. Winds along the wind prone areas of Wyoming Boulevard south of Casper, Wyoming, had frequent gusts above 58 mph with a maximum gust of 73 mph. The higher elevations of the Green Mountains also had strong winds with a maximum gust of 63 mph at the Fales Rock RAWS site.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds blew throughout much of Natrona County. Some of the stronger gusts included 75 mph at the Natrona County airport and 73 mph along Wyoming Boulevard on the south side of Casper, Wyoming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid level winds mixed to the surface ahead of a cold front passage and brought high winds to the wind prone areas of central Wyoming. In central Wyoming, the Green Mountains, Eastern Wind River Range and Natrona County all saw wind gusts as high as 75 mph. A wind gust of 61 mph was reported in Clark in Park County, Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor at the Red Canyon reported wind gusts as high as 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another shortwave moved through western Wyoming and brought heavy snow to portions of the area. The heaviest snow fell in the favored upslope areas of the Tetons where 22 inches was recorded at Grand Targhee. In the Star Valley, favorable northwest flow brought 13 inches of snow at Alpine. Snow amounts dropped sharply further south with only 2 inches at Afton, Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell through much of the Tetons. The highest amount was 22 inches at the Grand Targhee SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged high wind event occurred across southeast Wyoming from 31 January through the 1st of February 2020. Strong winds combined with recent snow fall to create a  multiple vehicle accident east of Laramie on 31 January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific trough moved into western Wyoming on New Years Day and brought heavy snow to many areas. Amounts over a foot were common in the mountains with close to 2 feet in the Tetons, including 21 inches of new snow at Jackson Hole, Wyoming. Heavy snow also fell in the valleys, with 13 inches of snow at Alpine, Wyoming and 6 to 12 inches around the Jackson Valley. Meanwhile, strong mid level winds were forced to the ground by favorable jet dynamics in the wind prone areas East of the Divide. Wind gusts over 70 mph were recorded near Clark, Wyoming with wind gusts of 66 mph spreading into the west side of Cody, Wyoming. Wind gusts of 75 mph were reported south of Casper, Wyoming in Natrona County as well with 60 mph winds at Jeffrey City, Wyoming. In and around Cody, Wyoming, damage was reported. This included damaged fences, a destroyed metal shed that landed on a car and a roof torn off of a trailer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow of 1 to 2 feet fell throughout much of the Tetons and Gros Ventre mountains. This included 21 inches at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front dropped slowly southward across Wyoming. The front combined with ample moisture, favorable jet dynamics and lee cyclogenesis over Colorado to bring heavy snow many areas East of the Continental Divide. The heaviest snow fell in the favored northeasterly upslope areas of Fremont and Natrona Counites. Near Lander, Wyoming, some locations reported 20 inches of snow with 10 to 16 inches around the city of Lander, Wyoming. Amounts around Riverton, Wyoming, ranged from 9 to 13 inches, with anywhere from 8 to 17 inches around Casper, Wyoming. The snow combined with winds gusting to around 50 mph at times, making travel nearly impossible for a time and bringing numerous road closures, including much of Interstates 25 and 80 for two days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in northern Johnson County. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 6 to 9 inches with locally higher amounts. The snow combined with winds gusting over 40 mph brought blowing and drifting snow that resulted in the closure of Interstates 25 and 90 for a time.

The heaviest snow occurred along the Beartooth/Absaroka and Big Horn Mountain Foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 15+ inches was reported. An avalanche occurred across Red Grade Road. The avalanche occurred approximately a half-mile above the springs in the burn area. No injuries were reported. However, six cabin owners were stranded until the debris was cleared off the road. In addition, one cabin owner reported 30-36 inches of snow during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact storm system brought snow to much of northeastern Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell across the Bear Lodge Mountains and Wyoming Black Hills, where ten to 18 inches were reported in many locations. The rest of northeastern Wyoming received two to eight inches of snow, with downslope areas of Weston County seeing very little snowfall. Winds gusting to 30 mph on the plains produced some blowing and drifting snow and caused hazardous travel.

The Richard Mountain fire begin southeast of Rock Springs on the afternoon of August 2 and spread rapidly to the south and east driven by strong winds. Although the area is sparsely populated, numerous oil and gas structures were threatened. The fire growth slowed around August 6th as lighter winds moved in and was contained by August 9th. A total of 7.632 acres was burned in Sweetwater County as well as in neighboring Utah.

A wildfire begin to south of Powder River and spread rapidly northeastward, eventually jumping US 20 26. The fire led to the shutdown of the road for several hours on the afternoon of August 13th. The fire was brought under control late in the evening of August 13th after burning 2,112 acres.

Very dry weather and lightning activity ignited the Lone Star fire in Yellowstone Park around 3 miles southeast of Yellowstone Park. The fire than spread southeastward and grew to around 1000 acres by August 31st. Please see September Storm Data for the conclusion of this fire.
A microburst at Winchester brought down tree limbs 5 to 7 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough and ample moisture moved into the western mountains and brought heavy amounts of snow. The highest amount was reported at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort where 19 inches of new snow fell on top of the mountain. Other notable amounts included 12 inches in southwestern Yellowstone, 8 inches in the town of Jackson, Wyoming and 13 inches near Smoot, Wyoming in the Star Valley. In central Wyoming, strong mid level winds were forced to the ground by a strong jet stream. Winds gusted as high as 84 mph to the east of Casper in Natrona County, Wyoming. Other strong wind gusts included 69 mph at the Riverton, Wyoming airport, 77 mph near Lander, Wyoming and 64 mph at the Sweetwater County airport near Rock Springs, Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of Yellowstone Park. The highest amount reported was 13 inches at the Lewis Lake SNOTEL site.

High wind gusts were felt across portions of Carbon, Albany, Converse and Platte Counties. A Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor along Interstate 80 at County Road 402 about three miles east-southeast of Elk Mountain measured the highest reported wind gust of 82 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported wind gust measured at the Douglas Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter storm impacted the region on the 8th and 9th bringing in mixed precipitation and accumulating snowfall. In addition in the wake of the precipitation, hard freezing temperatures brought an end to the growing season with many locations dropping overnight temperatures into the middle 20s. Cheyenne set a new daily snowfall amount and tied earliest accumulating snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer located 1.1 miles north-northeast of Centennial measured a storm snowfall total of 12.3 inches.

Other observers in the area also measured heavy snowfall totals over 6.0 inches. Due to near-blizzard-like conditions, Interstate 80 was closed for several hours to allow for plows to clear the road to ensure safe travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow winds from thunderstorms became rather strong as the moved into Big Horn County on the evening of August 3rd. Wind gusts of 64 and 58 mph were reported at Cowley and Greybull, respectively. During the morning hours, outflow winds from a shower north of Jackson caused three balloons to make a rough landing, sending 11 people to hospital, including one who had to be evacuated by helicopter to Idaho Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow thunderstorm winds brought wind gusts of 64 mph to Cowley and 58 mph to the Greybull airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow winds from thunderstorms became rather strong as the moved into Big Horn County on the evening of August 3rd. Wind gusts of 64 and 58 mph were reported at Cowley and Greybull, respectively. During the morning hours, outflow winds from a shower north of Jackson caused three balloons to make a rough landing, sending 11 people to hospital, including one who had to be evacuated by helicopter to Idaho Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early morning, high based showers developed over the Tetons and moved into the Jackson Valley. A downdraft off of one of the shower caused 3 balloons to crash to the ground south of Teton Village. People were thrown from the baskets with the hard landings. In total 11 people went to the hospital, with 4 who were admitted and 1 that had to be evacuated by helicopter to a hospital in Idaho Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A abnormally strong ridge and dry soil brought record heat to western and central Wyoming. Many areas saw record high temperatures, with a few stations reaching 100 degrees. All time monthly high temperatures were set or tied at Big Piney, Casper, Lander, Riverton, Rock Springs and Worland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An all time September record high temperature of 91 degrees was set at Big Piney, Wyoming. This broke the old record of 87 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Swinging Bridge Fire started south of Jackson, Wyoming during the afternoon of September 6th. The fire spread rapidly and threatened three subdivisions in the area that had to be evacuated. Evacuations were lifted the next day as the fire was brought under control. Cooler weather and snow helped extinguish the fire by September 9th. A total of 102 acres was burned in the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see episode for the description.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass dropped southward behind a cold front that moved across Wyoming. The front combined with ample moisture as well as strong mid and upper levels winds to bring an historic early season storm to much of western and central Wyoming. Many areas East of the Continental Divide, especially in the higher elevations and adjacent foothills, saw large amounts of snow. The highest amounts fell in the Wind River Range and Casper Mountain where up to 18 inches of new snow fell. Many areas around Casper and Lander, Wyoming, saw up to a foot of snow. The heavy, wet snow fell on trees with full foliage and brought down branches, limbs and in some cases entire trees. The trees fell on power lines causing numerous power outages. In western and southern Wyoming, wind was the main hazard. The strongest winds occurred in Sweetwater County, with wind gusts as high as 86 mph reported at the Rock Springs airport. Numerous trees were downed in Rock Springs and Green River, leading to thousands losing power. Portions of Teton and Sublette Counties also saw widespread tree damage and loss of power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Timber Creek SNOTEL site recorded 12 inches of snow.

The snow fell on trees with full foliage and combined with gusty north winds brought down numerous limbs that led to almost 3000 customers to lose power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mullen Fire began on Thursday, September 17th in dense timber both litter and understory in the Medicine Bow National Forest under ideal fire weather conditions of low humidity, gusty winds, and critical fuel levels. Plenty of downed trees and recent beetle kill made the area a tinder box as several days through the end of September met Red Flag Warning conditions for critical fire weather and saw extreme growth for the fire to grow over 106,000 acres by the end of September. For communities of lower Keystone, Lake Creek and Foxborough, twenty-nine dwellings and thirty-one outbuildings were lost to the fire. On the last day of September extreme fire growth spread the fire down into Colorado. The fire continued to burn into October and November. Sixty-six total structures including homes and outbuildings were destroyed from the wildfire. Overall fire fighting cost $42.4 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across the region, bringing rain to the area initially. Unusually cold air moved in with the system and allowed rain to turn over to snow during the nighttime hours across elevations near or above 4000 feet and especially above 4500 feet. Snowfall amounts of four to eight inches were noted in some areas above 4500 feet, including the Sundance area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient and strong mid level winds mixed to the surface around Rock Springs and Green River, Wyoming. In addition, up to 7 inches of snow fell on trees that had full foliage. The result was widespread tree damage with large limbs and entire trees downed. At one point, almost the entire city of Green River along with much of Rock Springs, Wyoming was without power. Winds gusted as high as 67 mph in Green River at the height of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Highway Patrol investigated a crash between a dump truck and pickup truck along Interstate 25 west five miles west of Douglas around milepost 145. Around 8:05 PM MDT, a 66-year old male was outside of his pickup truck on the shoulder after sliding off the interstate when a dump truck lost control on the ice and snow-covered roadway and slid off the road into the individual and pickup truck, rolling over on its side with the individual pinned between the vehicles. The 66-year old male died as a result of the crash while the dump truck driver suffered no injuries. A public individual reported 6.0 inches of heavy snowfall a mile south of Douglas. Another unrelated accident occurred earlier in the evening out in Albany County by Rock River where a vehicle lost control on icy roadways crashing into another vehicle resulting in a death of an 18-year-old female and injuring four others.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mullen Fire continued to burn across the Medicine Bow National Forest through the month of October. Several late season Red Flag Warnings were issued for the zone for critical fire weather conditions that led to the wildfire spreading into Colorado at the end of September. The wildfire damaged 66 structures including both homes and outbuildings and burned 176,878 acres total, 156,823 acres in Wyoming. Wildfire was at 96 percent perimeter contained as of Halloween with minimal burning and smoldering continuing into November. Overall fire fighting cost $42.4 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the aftermath of strong low pressure system and fresh snowpack, cold Arctic air tanked overnight temperatures the morning of the 27th into the minus 20 and 30 ranges. Laramie Airport measured minimum wind chill of minus 32 while setting a new low temperature for the 27th of minus 26 breaking the previous record of minus 16. This also sets a new monthly low temperature for Laramie breaking the old record of minus 18 set back on 30 October 1993. Cheyenne broke a low temperature record while Rawlins tied their previous record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor along Wyoming Highway 230 near Skyline measured wind chills below minus 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved southeastward across the area and brought heavy snow to portions of western Wyoming. The highest amounts included 15 inches at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort and 16 inches at Pahaska, Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the Cody Foothills. Around nine inches of snow fell to the west of Cody with 12 inches at Pahaska. Amounts dropped off fairly rapidly further north and east with around three inches in and around Cody, Wyoming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a very cold airmass, a clear sky and fresh snow cover brought very cold temperatures to portions of western and central Wyoming. Many record lows for the date were set. Some all time monthly record lows were also set. Most impressive was at Worland, Wyoming, where the temperatures fell to 18 below zero, breaking the old monthly record by 14 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the Jackson Valley fell as low as 19 below zero.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the lower elevations fell as low as 15 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tight pressure gradients ahead of a deepening low pressure system and subsequent snowfall aided in the production of high winds across southeastern Wyoming, particularly the Interstate 80 corridor from Rawlins east to Buford, west of Cheyenne. Union Pacific Railroad sensor EMKAYW measured a peak wind gust of 72 mph. Several Wyoming Department of Transportation sensors along both Interstate 80 and 25 had measured wind gusts greater than 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Union Pacific Railroad sensor EMKAYW located fourteen miles west-southwest of Cheyenne measured the reported wind gust. A Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor at Otto or sixteen miles west-southwest of Cheyenne along Interstate 80 also measured one hour plus sustained winds of 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season arctic airmass arrived into the Billings Forecast Area during the 3rd week of the month. At the same time, a very strong upper level disturbance within an unstable northwest flow aloft was situated over south central Montana and northern Wyoming. The combination of these factors brought heavy, wet snow to a large portion of the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 7 to 14 inches was reported across the area. Roads south of Sheridan were black ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy snow fell across much of southeast Wyoming the evening of 24 October through much of 25 October. Heavy snow lead to extended road closures on Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter two miles southwest of Pine Bluffs reported 7.0 inches of snow. Interstate 80 westbound was closed after a pileup involving at least ten semi trucks and five vehicles near the Burns exit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought significant early season snowfall to parts of northeastern Wyoming. The highest snowfall amounts were over the Bear Lodge Mountains and adjacent plains, where six to 12 inches were reported. Elsewhere, amounts of two to six inches were reported. Unseasonably cold air was in place, especially behind the system, with record or near record temperatures noted.

Another early season arctic airmass moved into the Billings Forecast Area toward the end of the month. At the same time, a strong upper level disturbance combined with moisture off the Pacific resulted in an overrunning event across south central Montana and portions of northern Wyoming. The combination of these factors brought another round of heavy, wet snow to a large portion of the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 9 inches was reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long stretch of high winds were felt across the southern wind prone areas of southeastern Wyoming particularly the Arlington and Elk Mountain regions to the Buford area along Interstate 80. A couple sites measured wind gusts into the low 70 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor at Bear Creek or eight miles south of Chugwater along Interstate 25 measured the period of sustained winds.

A long duration derecho tracked northeast across Wyoming, producing wind gusts as high as 80 mph across much of northeastern Wyoming. Trees and power poles were downed by the wind, with minor property damage as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) with an embedded supercell structure|developed across far SE Carbon County around 2:45pm on 6/6/2020, and moved|across western and northern portions of the Snowy Mountain Range. The overall|radar presentation of the co-located cyclonic rotation at the leading edge of|the reflectivity surge improved near 2:50-2:56pm per the radar scans from KCYS.|The first note of tree damage from the tornado occurred on Forest Service Road 115 |(FS115) that ran west of the main Forest Service Road 100 (FS100) at an elevation |around 9200 feet. Sporadic softwood pine trees were uprooted along FS115 and fell to |the N/NW direction. The survey team continued north on FS100, and additional tree |branches and smaller pine trees were uprooted. Progressing farther north along FS100, |tree damage became more widespread and severe with degree of damage indicators increasing |as more snapped softwood pine trees were cataloged and noted. This portion of FS100 |decreases in elevation and into a narrow localized valley along Turpin Creek to the north |and northwest. A vantage point at the south end of the valley, higher in elevation,|allowed for a landscape view towards the main tree damage path. This enhanced visual|perspective allowed the survey team to see east-northeast uprooted and bent|softwood pine trees. There were also snapped pine trees down the main center line of the damage path oriented to the north to north-northwest, and softwood pine trees to the east of the main centerline, converging back to the west to northwest. It was noted the greatest tree damage on the south to middle portions of the local Turpin Creek Valley. This damage was located along the west side of FS100, crossed FS100, with the greatest snapping to the East as one progressed farther north along FS100. This evidence indicated the tornado crossed FS100 in following the main snapping of tree damage along with the notable convergent pattern of tree fall. From the south side of the local Valley, tree damage extended at least 100-200 yards west of the road, with a wider damage swath noted to the east of FS100 as one progressed farther north. The damage swath of uprooted and snapped trees east of FS100 was approximately 200-300 yards from the road. The damage area narrowed substantially and became indiscernible as the team progressed north towards Bow River Campground near the intersection of FS100 and FS101. The end point was marked at the north end of a concentrated area of tree debris, small tree uprooting, and dislodged branches at an elevation around 9000 feet.||It should be noted that sporadic tree damage was also noted both southwest and northwest of the main track and the concentrated damage area. Sporadic softwood pine uprooting was noted along FS100 just north of HWY 130, but showed no conclusive evidence of a converging pattern. Likewise, FS105, FS120, and FS111 also had sporadic trees down, but once again, all trees fell in a northerly direction. The uniform direction suggested straight-line winds were the likely culprit of this tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the Wind River Mountains and became severe as the moved northward as they tapped into strong mid level winds. Numerous official reporting stations indicated wind gusts past 58 mph in Fremont, Natrona and Johnson Counties. The highest wind gust reported was 62 mph at the Buffalo, Wyoming airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 58 mph and 59 mph wind gusts at the Lander and Riverton ASOS sites, respectively. High gusts occurred in the mountains with several 76 mph wind gusts at Red Canyon near Highway 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of unusually high dew points, ample shear, an approaching trough and strong wind levels winds mixing to the surface brought a rare derecho to portions of southern and central Wyoming. There were widespread reports of wind gusts over 58 mph in portions of Fremont, Natrona, Johnson, Park and Natrona Counties. Some wind gusts over 70 mph were reported as well. For the most part, little damage was reported. However, on the backside of Casper Mountain, around 30 trees were downed by the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor along Bitter Creek along Interstate 80 reported several wind gusts over 60 mph. The highest wind gust reported was 62 mph.

Heavy rain and snow melt raised the levels of the Wind River across central and eastern portions of Fremont County. The Wind River near Kinnear, Wyoming crested around 10.3 feet, 0.3 feet above flood stage around 11 pm on July 29th. At Riverton, Wyoming, the Wind River crested at 9.2 feet, 0.2 feet above flood stage on the evening of the 30th before retreating. In both cases, the flooding was only in low lying areas near the river and caused no damage.

A cold front moved across western and central Wyoming. Strong mid level winds mixed to the surface and brought strong wind to portions of the area. The highest wind gust was 79 mph reported along Highway 28 in the Wind River Mountains. Wind gusts past 60 mph were also reported in Park, Johnson, Big Horn, Natrona and Washakie counties in central and western Wyoming. Many roads were impacted as roads were closed to high profile vehicles for a time. On the back side of the storm, and area of snow developed near Casper, Wyoming and brought local amounts over six inches, especially closer to Casper Mountain in Natrona county, Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 67 mph was reported at the Worland, Wyoming airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the Tetons. The highest amount reported was 15 inches at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort in Teton county, Wyoming, 15 inches of new snow was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A person riding a snowmobile triggered an avalanche. He went under the avalanche twice before being split out at the base of it. The rider reported no injuries.

A cold front dropped southward across Wyoming and brought high winds to northern Johnson County, Wyoming. The maximum wind gusts reported were 68 mph at the Johnson County airport and 63 mph at Kearney Divide along Interstate 90.
Strong gap flow developed with a cold frontal passage and brought high winds to areas near Cody and Clark, Wyoming. Wind gusts as high as 66 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts as high as 66 mph were reported in the vicinity of Cody and Clark, Wyoming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across Wyoming. The disturbance tightened the pressure gradient across the area. In addition, favorable jet dynamics mixed strong mid level winds to the surface and brought high winds to much of the area. Many locations had wind gusts over 58 mph with several locations having hurricane force wind gusts. Some of the more notable gusts included 91 mph along the Chief Joseph Highway, 79 mph at Buffalo, Wyoming, 75 mph south of Casper, Wyoming and 66 mph at the Rock Springs airport. At Cody, Wyoming, numerous trees were downed, some up to two to three feet in diameter. One tree fell on a house, causing extensive roof damage. The strong winds led to transportation problems as well with road closures and bans on high profile vehicles, including along Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of high wind occurred along the Chief Joseph Highway in Park County, Wyoming. Sustained winds of over 60 mph occurred for several hours. There were also numerous wind gusts over 75 mph including a maximum gust of 91 mph.
